summary: DHH recounts a tense encounter with U.S. customs officers who became suspicious when he tried to explain that he develops open source software. What started as casual conversation escalated into aggressive questioning about his income and occupation, as the officers couldn't comprehend the concept of free software as a legitimate profession. He was flagged for a second-round interview and had to convince them he wasn't breaking any laws. The experience left him concluding that explaining open source to customs is a losing proposition.
key_points: U.S. customs officers treated open source software development as a suspicious activity requiring aggressive interrogation
The officers demanded specific income figures and couldn't understand the consulting/training business model around free software
The customs interaction escalated from casual conversation to a hostile second-round interview
DHH contrasts the hostile customs officers with the professional, routine behavior of other security personnel
money_quotes: Don't try explain open source to U.S. customs and immigration officer.
Developing free software is obviously a suspicious activity that needs to be examined with tough questions and a highly uncomfortable tone.
I was foolishly thinking that we were having a friendly yet polite chit-chat session where they feel you out. Wrong was I.
It was definitely an ordeal straightening out that I wasn't breaking any U.S. laws by contracting and developing open source software.
What a horrible customer service experience: Welcome to the United States!
key_insight: The concept of open source software is so foreign to bureaucratic authority figures that trying to explain it to customs officers will only invite suspicion and hostility.
